blocks.statement: I can predict constructive vs. destructive interference based on whether two waves are in phase, and identify a standing wave as the interference of a wave with its reflection.

blocks.whatWeKnow: Modern radar uses INTERFEROMETRY — combining signals from multiple receivers to resolve fine asteroid details much smaller than a single antenna could see.
blocks.connection: Day 9 builds on standing waves to explain how guitar strings + organ pipes (and Earth's seismic resonances on Day 17) work.

blocks.markdown:
## When two waves meet, they ADD

At every point, the displacements of overlapping waves simply **add**:

- **IN PHASE** (crest meets crest) → **CONSTRUCTIVE** interference. Net amplitude doubles.
- **OUT OF PHASE by λ/2** (crest meets trough) → **DESTRUCTIVE** interference. Net amplitude = 0.

No energy is destroyed in the quiet spots — it's redistributed to the loud spots.

**Standing waves** are the star application: a wave interferes with **its own reflection** off a fixed end. The result looks frozen in place — fixed **NODES** (no motion) and **ANTINODES** (max motion). A guitar string fixed at both ends can only host standing waves that FIT: nodes at both ends. The longest one that fits (one antinode in the middle) is the **fundamental**, with frequency

**f₁ = v / (2L)** — and harmonics at f_n = n·v/(2L).

terms.definition: When two waves meet, their displacements ADD at each point. Same-direction crests → bigger crest (CONSTRUCTIVE). Opposite displacements → cancellation (DESTRUCTIVE). Two stones dropped in a pond — ripples meet and create bright + dark patterns. Noise-canceling headphones produce a sound exactly opposite to incoming noise — they CANCEL.
terms.cognate: Sp. interferencia · Pt. interferência · HC entèfewans

terms.definition: Two waves IN PHASE: crests align with crests, troughs with troughs. Net displacement = sum (bigger). Two speakers playing the same tone reinforce each other at certain spots = LOUD. In phase doesn't mean identical — it means crests align in TIME and SPACE.
terms.cognate: Sp. interferencia constructiva · Pt. interferência construtiva

terms.definition: Two waves OUT OF PHASE by half a wavelength: crests align with troughs. Net displacement = 0 (cancellation). Noise-canceling headphones detect incoming noise and play the EXACT OPPOSITE — they cancel at your eardrum.
terms.cognate: Sp. interferencia destructiva · Pt. interferência destrutiva

terms.definition: A wave interfering with its own reflection. Produces fixed NODES (no motion) and ANTINODES (max motion). The wave LOOKS stationary. A guitar string fixed at both ends: pluck it and a standing wave forms — the fundamental has nodes at the ends and one antinode in the middle.
terms.cognate: Sp. onda estacionaria · Pt. onda estacionária · HC vag estasyonè

blocks.title: Interference doesn't destroy energy
blocks.markdown: In destructive zones, the energy is just shifted to constructive zones elsewhere. At dark fringes the energy is zero; at bright fringes it's doubled. And a standing wave is REAL motion of the medium — it just doesn't TRANSLATE left-right; it oscillates up-down at fixed locations.

blocks.instruction: TOP: two waves IN PHASE — sum. BOTTOM: two waves OUT OF PHASE — sum. Mark the amplitudes (in phase = double, out of phase = zero).
prompts: Top panel: two identical sines aligned, then their sum (2A).
Bottom panel: same sines shifted by λ/2, then their sum (flat zero).

blocks.prompt: Incoming engine noise enters as a 200 Hz wave with amplitude A = 1 (relative units). The headphones detect it and produce a 200 Hz wave with amplitude A = 1, but SHIFTED by half a wavelength. What's the net amplitude at your eardrum?
blocks.given: Both waves: 200 Hz, A = 1. Headphone wave shifted by λ/2 (opposite phase).
blocks.equation: A_net = A₁ + A₂ (where A₂ is OPPOSITE-signed)
blocks.work:
At every instant: A_net = (+1) + (−1) = 0
Continuously for both waves → silence at the eardrum.
blocks.answer: A_net = 0 → silence. (Headphones never PERFECTLY cancel — but ~30 dB attenuation is real.)

blocks.prompt: A guitar string is 0.65 m long. The wave speed along the string is 80 m/s. What's the fundamental (lowest) standing-wave frequency?
blocks.answer: f₁ ≈ 61.5 Hz. The second harmonic (n = 2) would be 123 Hz, third 184.5, etc.

blocks.prompt: PIPE ORGAN — A 1.0-m-long pipe organ tube, open at both ends, supports standing waves. Sound in air is 343 m/s. What's the fundamental frequency? (Use f₁ = v/(2L).)
blocks.equationHint: f₁ = v/(2L) — divide the speed by twice the length.

blocks.title: Geometry sets the song
blocks.markdown: The frequencies a guitar string + pipe organ + bell + Earth's crust + even an asteroid PRODUCE are set by their geometry — which standing waves fit. That's why a long string has a low fundamental and a short string a high one.

blocks.prompt: Noise-canceling headphones produce a sound wave OPPOSITE to incoming noise. Why does this cancel? Where does the noise energy go? Answer in two sentences.
blocks.frame: Two waves in phase produce ___ interference. Two waves out of phase by λ/2 produce ___ interference. A standing wave is what happens when a wave interferes with its own ___.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **25.7–25.8** (interference patterns and standing waves: nodes and antinodes). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **31.3–31.5** (interference of light: Young's double-slit experiment, diffraction gratings, and thin-film colors). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
